quiz.question: The author's preface explained why she had spent ten years writing the book. Where is the preface found?
options: At the end of the book, after the final chapter
At the beginning of the book, before the main text
In the middle of the book, between two major sections
On the dust jacket, alongside the publisher's summary
quiz.answer: At the beginning of the book, before the main text
quiz.explanation:
A preface is an introductory section written by the author, placed before the main text.
• preface = an author's introduction to their own book
• From Latin: 'praefari' — to say beforehand
• Distinct from a foreword (written by someone else) and an introduction (part of the argument)

quiz.question: He tried to efface all memories of the humiliation from his mind. What does 'efface' mean?
options: To suppress and eliminate completely
To revisit and come to terms with
To share with others to reduce their power
To channel into something more productive
quiz.answer: To suppress and eliminate completely
quiz.explanation:
To efface means to wipe out or make disappear — to erase from existence or memory.
• efface = to rub out; to make invisible or insignificant
• From French: 'effacer' — to erase the face of
• 'Self-effacing' = making oneself inconspicuous; retiring and modest

quiz.question: The guide warned that pickpocketing in the area had become commonplace. What does 'commonplace' mean here?
options: It had become visible and easy to witness in the area, as the evidence showed
It had become very widespread and no longer surprising
It had become the subject of formal police reports
It had been targeted by a new anti-crime initiative
quiz.answer: It had become very widespread and no longer surprising
quiz.explanation:
Commonplace means ordinary and unremarkable — occurring so often it no longer surprises.
• commonplace = so common as to be unremarkable; ordinary
• From 'common place' — a topic found everywhere in rhetoric
• 'A commonplace observation' = something so often said it has lost impact

quiz.question: The flood displaced thousands of families from their homes. What does 'displace' mean?
options: To destroy the homes so they could not be returned to
To force people to leave their usual place
To damage property beyond the possibility of repair
To require emergency evacuation of an entire region
quiz.answer: To force people to leave their usual place
quiz.explanation:
To displace means to force from the usual position — to move something out of its place.
• displace = to move from the normal position; to force out
• From Latin: 'dis-' + 'place'
• 'Displaced persons' = refugees; 'displace a leader' = force them from power

quiz.question: The basket was made by interlacing strips of willow together. What does 'interlace' mean?
options: To glue strips together using a natural adhesive
To weave or cross strands over and under each other
To press strips flat and bind them at the edges
To carve patterns into the surface of woven material
quiz.answer: To weave or cross strands over and under each other
quiz.explanation:
To interlace means to weave or cross things together — alternating over and under.
• interlace = to arrange in a crisscross or interwoven pattern
• From 'inter-' (between) + 'lace' (to thread)
• 'Interlaced fingers', 'interlaced threads' — crossing and interweaving

quiz.question: The government's decision was unpopular with the wider populace. What does 'populace' mean?
options: The political opposition and its supporters
The ordinary people of a nation or area
The voting public at a national election
The most vocal and politically active citizens
quiz.answer: The ordinary people of a nation or area
quiz.explanation:
The populace is the general population — the ordinary people of a place.
• populace = the common people; the general public
• From Latin: 'populus' — the people
• Distinct from 'population' (a statistical count) — populace implies social identity

quiz.question: The dog was declared a menace to the neighborhood after several attacks. What is a menace?
options: A serious and recurring public nuisance or danger
An animal subject to a formal complaint from residents
A creature that causes fear but rarely causes harm
A problem that requires intervention from the authorities
quiz.answer: A serious and recurring public nuisance or danger
quiz.explanation:
A menace is a person, animal, or thing that poses a threat or danger.
• menace = a threat; something dangerous and threatening
• From Latin: 'minaciae' — threatening gestures
• 'A menace to society', 'menacing behavior' — implies ongoing danger

quiz.question: The blacksmith's furnace burned at over 1,000 degrees Celsius. What is a furnace?
options: A large enclosed fire used for producing intense heat
A pressurized vessel for heating water to make steam
An insulated chamber used for drying materials slowly, as the evidence showed
A gas-powered heating system for domestic use
quiz.answer: A large enclosed fire used for producing intense heat
quiz.explanation:
A furnace is an enclosed structure for producing very high temperatures.
• furnace = an enclosed chamber for burning fuel at very high temperatures
• From Latin: 'fornax' — oven, furnace
• Used in metalworking, glassmaking, and for home heating (furnace = boiler)

quiz.question: The tortoise retreated entirely into its carapace. What is a carapace?
options: The soft underbelly of a shelled animal
The hard upper shell covering the back of a turtle or crustacean
The outer layer of skin on the legs of a tortoise, as the evidence showed
The horny covering over the beak of a reptile
quiz.answer: The hard upper shell covering the back of a turtle or crustacean
quiz.explanation:
A carapace is the hard upper shell of a turtle, tortoise, or crustacean.
• carapace = the bony or chitinous dorsal covering of certain animals
• From Spanish: 'carapacho' — shell
• Figuratively: 'a carapace of cynicism' = a protective outer shell of attitude

quiz.question: She told the passengers to brace for impact. What does 'brace' mean here?
options: To lean forward and hold on tightly
To prepare and make oneself firm against a coming force
To pull a seatbelt tight across the body
To adopt the crash position with head between the knees, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To prepare and make oneself firm against a coming force
quiz.explanation:
To brace means to prepare by making oneself firm — to steady against impact.
• brace = to make firm; to prepare for something difficult or shocking
• From Old French: 'brace' — arms (supporting and strengthening)
• 'Brace yourself', 'brace for impact' — steeling against what is coming

quiz.question: The government embraced the new technology enthusiastically. What does 'embrace' mean here?
options: It funded research into the technology's development
It adopted and accepted it wholeheartedly
It commissioned an independent review of the technology
It regulated the technology before permitting widespread use
quiz.answer: It adopted and accepted it wholeheartedly
quiz.explanation:
To embrace means to accept or adopt willingly — to take something fully to oneself.
• embrace = to accept enthusiastically; to take up willingly
• From 'em-' + 'brace' (arms) — to put one's arms around something
• Literally: to hug; figuratively: to welcome and take in fully

quiz.question: The farmers carved terraces into the hillside to grow rice. What are terraces in this context?
options: Channels cut to direct rainwater down the slope
Flat, stepped platforms cut into a slope for cultivation
Walls built to prevent soil from washing down the hill
Plots of land cleared of vegetation at the top of the hill
quiz.answer: Flat, stepped platforms cut into a slope for cultivation
quiz.explanation:
A terrace is a raised flat area — especially one cut into a hillside for farming.
• terrace = a flat ledge cut into a slope; a level platform
• From Latin: 'terra' — earth
• 'Rice terraces', 'terrace house' — the idea of a level platform on a slope

quiz.question: The company was accused of fleecing its customers by charging hidden fees. What does 'fleece' mean here?
options: To overcharge or swindle someone out of their money
To deceive customers about the quality of a product
To advertise misleadingly to attract new clients
To charge fees that were not disclosed until after purchase
quiz.answer: To overcharge or swindle someone out of their money
quiz.explanation:
To fleece means to swindle — to take money from someone by deception or overcharging.
• fleece = to defraud; to strip someone of their money
• From shearing a sheep of its wool — taking everything
• 'Get fleeced' = be cheated and left with nothing

quiz.question: Long experience had given the reporter a jaundice toward official statements. What does 'jaundice' mean here?
options: A comprehensive and evidence-based skepticism
A bitter and disillusioned distrust of official claims
A professional reluctance to publish unverified material
A tendency to investigate statements before reporting them
quiz.answer: A bitter and disillusioned distrust of official claims
quiz.explanation:
Jaundice (figuratively) means a bitterly cynical or envious outlook — a distorted, sour view.
• jaundice = a state of bitterness or cynicism that distorts judgment
• From the disease jaundice — which yellows the skin and was thought to distort perception
• 'A jaundiced view' = colored by bitterness and disappointment

quiz.question: The judge dismissed the case to avoid prejudicing the jury. What does 'prejudice' mean here?
options: To bore the jury with repetitive procedural arguments
To influence the jury's opinion before proper deliberation
To introduce inadmissible evidence at an early stage
To delay the trial beyond the time the jury was available, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To influence the jury's opinion before proper deliberation
quiz.explanation:
To prejudice means to influence unfairly in advance — to cause pre-judgment.
• prejudice = a preconceived opinion not based on evidence; to harm by prior bias
• From Latin: 'praejudicium' — prior judgment
• As a noun: a prejudice is an unfair bias; as a verb: to prejudice = to harm or bias

quiz.question: The cathedral was the most impressive edifice in the city. What is an edifice?
options: A historically significant monument or landmark
A large, imposing building
A building set aside for religious purposes only
A structure built to last many centuries
quiz.answer: A large, imposing building
quiz.explanation:
An edifice is a large, impressive building — especially a grand or imposing one.
• edifice = a large building; often grand or imposing
• From Latin: 'aedificare' — to build
• Figurative: 'the edifice of law' = an elaborate structure of rules and institutions

quiz.question: The doctor examined the orifice to check for blockage. What is an orifice?
options: An opening in the body through which substances pass
A valve that regulates the flow of fluids in the body
A cavity within a bone or organ used for storage
A channel connecting two internal organs
quiz.answer: An opening in the body through which substances pass
quiz.explanation:
An orifice is an opening — especially a natural one in the body.
• orifice = a natural opening in the body, such as the mouth or nostril
• From Latin: 'orificium' — from 'os' (mouth) + 'facere' (to make)
• Also used in engineering: an orifice in a pipe = a controlled opening

quiz.question: The magician's performance was pure artifice — clever but entirely constructed. What does 'artifice' mean?
options: A natural talent displayed through technical skill
Clever trickery and cunning construction — skill used to deceive
A performance requiring years of dedicated practice
An illusion created with the help of advanced technology, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Clever trickery and cunning construction — skill used to deceive
quiz.explanation:
Artifice means cunning contrivance — skill employed to trick or deceive.
• artifice = clever trickery; the use of skill or ingenuity to deceive
• From Latin: 'artificium' — craftsmanship; from 'ars' + 'facere' (to make)
• 'An artifice' = a trick; 'artificial' shares the root — both are made, not natural

quiz.question: The priest raised the chalice during the service. What is a chalice?
options: A large ornamental plate used in religious ceremonies
A goblet or cup used in religious ceremonies
A cloth used to cover the altar during services
An incense burner swung during processions
quiz.answer: A goblet or cup used in religious ceremonies
quiz.explanation:
A chalice is a large cup or goblet — especially one used in Christian communion.
• chalice = a ceremonial cup, especially the one used in the Eucharist
• From Latin: 'calix' — cup
• 'A poisoned chalice' = something that looks good but brings harm

quiz.question: She was convicted as an accomplice to the robbery even though she only drove the getaway car. What is an accomplice?
options: A person who plans a crime but does not take part in carrying it out
A person who helps another commit a crime
A person who witnesses a crime and fails to report it
A person who benefits from a crime without knowing about it
quiz.answer: A person who helps another commit a crime
quiz.explanation:
An accomplice is a person who helps someone else commit a crime.
• accomplice = a person who assists another in committing a crime
• From French: 'complice' — an associate in crime
• Distinct from the principal (the main perpetrator) — the accomplice assists

quiz.question: The editor spliced two sections of film together. What does 'splice' mean?
options: To layer one section of film over another
To join two lengths by overlapping and fastening the ends
To cut a section cleanly from the middle of a reel
To duplicate a section to fill a gap in the footage, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To join two lengths by overlapping and fastening the ends
quiz.explanation:
To splice means to join two ends together by overlapping and fastening.
• splice = to join by interweaving or overlapping the ends
• From Dutch: 'splissen' — to splice a rope
• 'Splice a rope', 'splice film', 'splice genes' — joining two things at their ends

quiz.question: The lawyer took a slice of the settlement for her fees. What does 'slice' mean here?
options: A surprisingly large portion of a total amount
A distinct share or portion of something
A fee calculated as a fixed rate regardless of outcome
An unexpected deduction made from a payment
quiz.answer: A distinct share or portion of something
quiz.explanation:
A slice means a portion or share cut from a larger whole.
• slice = a part cut or taken from a larger whole
• From Old French: 'esclice' — a splinter
• 'A slice of the profits', 'a slice of life' — a portion taken from something bigger

quiz.question: The restorer carefully repaired the painted cornice running around the ceiling. What is a cornice?
options: A decorative strip at the junction of wall and ceiling
An arch over a doorway or window in a historic building
The ornamental frame surrounding a painted mural
The raised platform from which a speaker addresses an audience
quiz.answer: A decorative strip at the junction of wall and ceiling
quiz.explanation:
A cornice is a decorative molding at the top of a wall where it meets the ceiling, or on a building's exterior.
• cornice = a projecting decorative molding along the top of a wall
• From Italian: 'cornice' — a frame, ledge
• In mountaineering: a cornice is an overhanging lip of snow on a ridge

quiz.question: The sommelier selected only choice wines for the tasting. What does 'choice' mean here?
options: Wines that offered the best value for money
Of the very best quality — carefully selected
Wines that had won recent international awards
Wines chosen to represent a variety of regions
quiz.answer: Of the very best quality — carefully selected
quiz.explanation:
Choice (as an adjective) means of the highest quality — the best available.
• choice = (adjective) of superior quality; carefully selected as the best
• From Old French: 'chois' — a choosing
• 'Choice cuts of meat', 'choice words' — the finest, most carefully picked

quiz.question: The nation rejoiced when the hostages were released. What does 'rejoice' mean?
options: To express relief that a difficult situation is over
To feel or show great joy and delight
To celebrate in an organized, public manner
To give thanks for a favorable outcome
quiz.answer: To feel or show great joy and delight
quiz.explanation:
To rejoice means to feel or show great joy — to be intensely glad.
• rejoice = to feel joy; to be delighted
• From Old French: 'rejoir' — to gladden again
• 'Rejoice in' = to take great pleasure in; 'cause for rejoicing' = reason to be glad

quiz.question: The climber froze when she realized she was standing on the edge of a precipice. What is a precipice?
options: A very steep, vertical rock face — a sheer drop
A narrow ledge on the side of a mountain
An overhang of rock jutting out over a valley
A slope so steep that climbing becomes impossible
quiz.answer: A very steep, vertical rock face — a sheer drop
quiz.explanation:
A precipice is a very steep rock face — a vertical or near-vertical cliff.
• precipice = a sheer drop; a vertical cliff face
• From Latin: 'praeceps' — headlong
• Figurative: 'on the precipice of disaster' = at the very edge of catastrophe

quiz.question: The event was held under the auspices of the United Nations. What does 'under the auspices of' mean?
options: With the financial support of that organization, as the evidence showed
With the patronage, protection, and authority of that body
With the formal approval of the member states
In the premises owned and operated by that body
quiz.answer: With the patronage, protection, and authority of that body
quiz.explanation:
Auspices means patronage and protection — the guiding authority under which something occurs.
• auspice = protection and sponsorship; a sign of what is to come
• From Latin: 'auspicium' — observation of birds for omens (augury)
• 'Under the auspices of' = with the backing and guidance of

quiz.question: His avarice led him to hoard his fortune rather than enjoy it. What is avarice?
options: An extreme and insatiable greed for wealth
A cautious approach to financial risk and spending
A tendency to accumulate wealth for a specific future purpose
A fear of poverty that drives excessive saving
quiz.answer: An extreme and insatiable greed for wealth
quiz.explanation:
Avarice is extreme greed for money or material gain.
• avarice = insatiable greed for riches; one of the seven deadly sins
• From Latin: 'avaritia' — greed
• 'Avaricious' = greedy; 'avarice' implies greed beyond all reasonable desire

quiz.question: She changed the menu on a caprice, annoying the chef who had planned everything. What is a caprice?
options: A firm decision made without consulting others, as the evidence showed
A sudden, impulsive change with no rational basis
A preference that she had held for a long time
A change made in response to a guest's request
quiz.answer: A sudden, impulsive change with no rational basis
quiz.explanation:
A caprice is a sudden, impulsive change of mind — a whim.
• caprice = a sudden change of mood or behavior; a whim
• From Italian: 'capriccio' — a sudden start, whim
• 'Capricious' = governed by whims; unpredictably changeable

quiz.question: The surgeon was sued for malpractice after a botched operation. What is malpractice?
options: Professional negligence or misconduct that causes harm
An operation that failed despite being performed correctly
A breach of the patient's confidentiality rights
An unauthorized procedure carried out without consent
quiz.answer: Professional negligence or misconduct that causes harm
quiz.explanation:
Malpractice is improper, negligent, or illegal professional conduct — especially by doctors or lawyers.
• malpractice = improper or negligent professional behavior that causes harm
• From Latin: 'mal-' (bad) + 'practice'
• 'Medical malpractice' = treatment falling below the required standard of care

quiz.question: She took on a young apprentice to learn the trade alongside her. What is an apprentice?
options: A junior employee completing a formal qualification
A person learning a trade under a skilled practitioner
A recent graduate starting their first professional role
A volunteer working in exchange for experience
quiz.answer: A person learning a trade under a skilled practitioner
quiz.explanation:
An apprentice is someone learning a trade by working under a skilled person.
• apprentice = a person bound to work for another in order to learn a trade
• From Old French: 'aprentis' — one who is learning
• Apprenticeships combine on-the-job training with formal learning

quiz.question: The armistice was signed on 11 November 1918, ending the fighting on the Western Front. What is an armistice?
options: A formal peace treaty ending a war permanently, as the evidence showed
An agreement to stop fighting while a peace deal is negotiated
A surrender by one side acknowledging defeat
A ceasefire imposed by a neutral third party
quiz.answer: An agreement to stop fighting while a peace deal is negotiated
quiz.explanation:
An armistice is a formal agreement to stop fighting — a ceasefire, not a final peace treaty.
• armistice = an agreement to stop hostilities temporarily
• From Latin: 'arma' (arms) + 'sistere' (to stop)
• An armistice halts the fighting; a peace treaty formally ends the war

quiz.question: Water seeped through the interstices in the stone wall. What are interstices?
options: Hairline fractures running through the interior of the stone, as the evidence showed
Small gaps or spaces between things — especially close-packed things
The weak points where one stone rests on another
The channels cut deliberately for drainage purposes
quiz.answer: Small gaps or spaces between things — especially close-packed things
quiz.explanation:
An interstice is a small gap or interval — the space between closely packed things.
• interstice = a small space between things; an intervening gap
• From Latin: 'interstitium' — a space between
• 'The interstices of the law' = the gaps in legislation

quiz.question: Roses climbed up the wooden lattice on the garden wall. What is a lattice?
options: A solid panel used to support climbing plants
A criss-cross framework of strips with spaces between
A series of horizontal bars fixed to a wall
An arch made of curved metal or wooden strands, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A criss-cross framework of strips with spaces between
quiz.explanation:
A lattice is a structure of crossed strips with regular gaps between them.
• lattice = a framework of crossed strips; a criss-cross pattern
• From Old French: 'latis' — lath
• 'Lattice window', 'crystal lattice' (molecular structure), 'lattice pastry' — always crosswork

quiz.question: The operator opened the sluice to allow water to flow through. What is a sluice?
options: A pump used to move water uphill
A gate or channel controlling the flow of water
A filter used to remove debris from flowing water
A reservoir holding water for controlled release
quiz.answer: A gate or channel controlling the flow of water
quiz.explanation:
A sluice is a sliding gate or artificial channel used to control the flow of water.
• sluice = a floodgate; a sliding gate controlling water flow
• From Latin: 'exclusa (aqua)' — excluded (water); water held back
• 'Sluice gate', 'sluice water' — to flood or wash through

quiz.question: The lizard disappeared into a crevice in the rock face. What is a crevice?
options: A narrow opening or crack in a rock or wall
A small cave behind a waterfall
An undercut at the base of a cliff
A hole left by the roots of a fallen tree, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A narrow opening or crack in a rock or wall
quiz.explanation:
A crevice is a narrow crack or fissure — especially in rock.
• crevice = a narrow crack or opening in a hard surface
• From Old French: 'crevace' — a crack
• 'Crevice' is narrower than a crevasse (a deep fissure in a glacier)

quiz.question: She was a complete novice on skis — she had never been on a slope before. What is a novice?
options: A person learning under the guidance of an instructor
A beginner with little or no experience
A person who lacks the aptitude to improve quickly
A person attempting a difficult activity for the first time
quiz.answer: A beginner with little or no experience
quiz.explanation:
A novice is a person new to an activity — without experience or training.
• novice = a beginner; someone new to a subject or activity
• From Latin: 'novicius' — newly arrived, new
• Also: a novice in a religious order is in the probationary period before taking vows

quiz.question: The harvest produced an abundance of fruit that year. What does 'abundance' mean?
options: A larger-than-expected yield compared to the previous year
A very large quantity — more than enough
A variety of different kinds of fruit from the same orchard
A surplus that could be stored for the winter months
quiz.answer: A very large quantity — more than enough
quiz.explanation:
Abundance means a very large supply — more than enough of something.
• abundance = a very large quantity; plentiful supply
• From Latin: 'abundare' — to overflow
• 'In abundance' = plentifully; 'abundant' = existing in large quantities

quiz.question: She returned from injury with a vengeance, scoring in every match. What does 'with a vengeance' mean?
options: With anger at those who had doubted her recovery
With great intensity and force — making up for lost time
With a desire to prove wrong those who had questioned her
With the determination not to be injured again
quiz.answer: With great intensity and force — making up for lost time
quiz.explanation:
'With a vengeance' means with great force or intensity — more powerfully than before.
• vengeance = punishment inflicted in return for a wrong; retribution
• 'With a vengeance' = with extreme force or thoroughness
• From Latin: 'vindicare' — to claim, avenge

quiz.question: The CEO's extravagance was criticized when the company was losing money. What does 'extravagance' mean?
options: Spending on unnecessary luxuries beyond what is appropriate
Using company funds for personal purposes
Making decisions without approval from the board
Focusing on short-term gains at the expense of stability, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Spending on unnecessary luxuries beyond what is appropriate
quiz.explanation:
Extravagance is excessive and wasteful spending — going far beyond what is reasonable.
• extravagance = spending far beyond what is justified; excess
• From Latin: 'extravagari' — to wander beyond
• 'Extravagant' = wildly excessive; 'extravaganza' = an elaborate, lavish spectacle

quiz.question: His arrogance made it impossible for him to accept advice from anyone. What is arrogance?
options: An exaggerated sense of one's own importance and superiority
A determined refusal to change one's position under pressure, as the evidence showed
A tendency to dismiss opinions from less experienced people
An inability to recognize one's own weaknesses and errors
quiz.answer: An exaggerated sense of one's own importance and superiority
quiz.explanation:
Arrogance is an exaggerated sense of one's own importance — overbearing pride.
• arrogance = offensive presumption; an inflated view of oneself
• From Latin: 'arrogare' — to claim for oneself without right
• 'Arrogant' = behaving as though superior to others

quiz.question: She answered the difficult questions with remarkable insouciance, as though nothing troubled her. What does 'insouciance' mean?
options: A carefully rehearsed appearance of calm under pressure
A casual lack of concern — a breezy indifference
A professional composure developed over years
A studied reluctance to show any emotion in public
quiz.answer: A casual lack of concern — a breezy indifference
quiz.explanation:
Insouciance is a casual, cheerful lack of concern — breezy indifference.
• insouciance = a relaxed unconcern; nonchalance
• From French: 'insouciant' — carefree
• 'With insouciance' = without a care in the world; the attitude is effortless and charming

quiz.question: The new citizens swore allegiance to the country. What did they pledge?
options: Obedience to the nation's laws
Loyalty and devotion to the country
Commitment to perform civic duties
Respect for the country's cultural traditions
quiz.answer: Loyalty and devotion to the country
quiz.explanation:
Allegiance is loyalty and devotion — a commitment owed to a person, group, or nation.
• allegiance = loyalty; the duty of a subject to their sovereign or nation
• From Old French: 'ligeance' — feudal loyalty
• 'Pledge allegiance', 'transfer one's allegiance' — loyalty that can be given or withheld

quiz.question: His dalliance with vegetarianism lasted about a week before he returned to his usual diet. What does 'dalliance' mean?
options: A brief, casual involvement with something without serious commitment
A failed attempt to adopt a new habit or lifestyle
A period of experiment before making a permanent decision, as the evidence showed
A superficial interest that was never seriously pursued
quiz.answer: A brief, casual involvement with something without serious commitment
quiz.explanation:
A dalliance is a casual or trifling engagement — without seriousness or commitment.
• dalliance = a brief flirtation with a person or idea; a trifling
• From 'dally' — to act or talk playfully without purpose
• 'A political dalliance', 'a romantic dalliance' — casual, uncommitted contact

quiz.question: The factory was shut down for failing to demonstrate compliance with safety regulations. What is compliance?
options: Awareness and understanding of applicable regulations
Conformity with and adherence to rules or requirements
Regular reporting of safety incidents to the regulator, as the evidence showed
Submission of documentation to an oversight body
quiz.answer: Conformity with and adherence to rules or requirements
quiz.explanation:
Compliance means acting in accordance with rules, laws, or standards.
• compliance = the action of conforming to a rule or order
• From Latin: 'complere' — to fill up, fulfill
• 'In compliance with', 'compliance officer' — meeting required standards

quiz.question: Her account was at variance with the official version of events. What does 'at variance' mean?
options: It was more detailed than the official account
It conflicted with and differed from the official account
It was less credible than the official version
It was consistent except on one minor factual point, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: It conflicted with and differed from the official account
quiz.explanation:
'At variance' means in disagreement or conflict — differing from something else.
• variance = the fact of being different or inconsistent
• From Latin: 'variare' — to vary
• 'At variance with' = in conflict or contradiction; 'statistical variance' = measure of spread

quiz.question: The surgeon lanced the abscess to drain it. What does 'lance' mean here?
options: To cut open a wound or swelling with a sharp instrument
To press firmly on a swelling to release pressure, as the evidence showed
To apply medicated cream to reduce inflammation
To numb an area before a surgical procedure
quiz.answer: To cut open a wound or swelling with a sharp instrument
quiz.explanation:
To lance means to cut open — to pierce with a lance or lancet to drain fluid.
• lance = to pierce or cut open (a boil or abscess) with a sharp instrument
• From Latin: 'lancea' — a spear
• A 'lancet' is the small surgical blade used for this

quiz.question: The new hire's caution counterbalanced her colleague's impulsive approach. What does 'counterbalance' mean?
options: It corrected the problems caused by her colleague's methods, as the evidence showed
It offset and neutralized the effect of the opposite quality
It created a productive tension within the team
It prevented her colleague from making costly errors
quiz.answer: It offset and neutralized the effect of the opposite quality
quiz.explanation:
To counterbalance means to offset — to act as an equal and opposite force.
• counterbalance = to act as a weight or force against another; to offset
• From 'counter-' (against) + 'balance'
• The two forces don't cancel — they create equilibrium

quiz.question: He accepted the award with complete nonchalance, as if it were nothing. What does 'nonchalance' mean?
options: A casual unconcern that borders on rudeness
A calm, relaxed indifference — apparent lack of concern
A quiet confidence earned through repeated success
A deliberate attempt to appear unaffected by praise, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A calm, relaxed indifference — apparent lack of concern
quiz.explanation:
Nonchalance is a calm, casual indifference — an easy lack of concern.
• nonchalance = casual unconcern; an easy manner
• From French: 'nonchaloir' — not to be warm; to be indifferent
• A 'nonchalant' person appears effortlessly unbothered

quiz.question: After the crisis, officials tried to restore some semblance of normality. What does 'semblance' mean?
options: The full restoration of conditions as they were before, as the evidence showed
An outward appearance of something — not necessarily the full reality
A temporary state that was expected to improve
The most basic elements of a functioning system
quiz.answer: An outward appearance of something — not necessarily the full reality
quiz.explanation:
A semblance is an outward appearance — the surface look of something, often incomplete.
• semblance = an apparent form; an outward impression
• From Old French: 'sembler' — to seem, resemble
• 'A semblance of order' = it looks like order, but may not truly be

quiz.question: In legal parlance, this behavior would be described as tortious. What does 'parlance' mean?
options: In the terminology and language used by a particular group
In the most formal and precise use of the word
In the view of a recognized authority on the subject, as the evidence showed
In the language of an international governing body
quiz.answer: In the terminology and language used by a particular group
quiz.explanation:
Parlance refers to the particular way language is used in a specific context or group.
• parlance = a way of speaking particular to a group; terminology
• From Old French: 'parler' — to speak
• 'In common parlance' = in ordinary speech; 'in medical parlance' = in medical terminology

quiz.question: He stormed out of the meeting in a fit of petulance when his proposal was rejected. What is petulance?
options: A childish sulkiness and irritability when one does not get one's way
An angry outburst directed at those responsible for a setback, as the evidence showed
A principled refusal to continue a discussion on unfair terms
An extreme emotional reaction to personal criticism
quiz.answer: A childish sulkiness and irritability when one does not get one's way
quiz.explanation:
Petulance is childish, sulky bad temper — pouting irritability.
• petulance = unreasonable irritability or sulkiness; immaturity in reaction
• From Latin: 'petulantia' — insolence, wantonness
• 'Petulant' = sulkily bad-tempered; petulance = the behavior itself

quiz.question: The army's sappers cleared unexploded ordnance from the area before civilians could return. What is ordnance?
options: Military weapons, artillery, and ammunition
Any object posing a potential danger to the public
Buried debris from a conflict that requires removal
Military equipment left behind after a battle
quiz.answer: Military weapons, artillery, and ammunition
quiz.explanation:
Ordnance is military weapons, ammunition, and artillery.
• ordnance = mounted guns, missiles, and military supplies
• From Old French: 'ordenance' — arrangement (of troops and weapons)
• Note: 'ordnance' (weapons) vs. 'ordinance' (a law or regulation) — often confused

quiz.question: As penance for missing her birthday, he cooked dinner for a week. What is penance?
options: An action performed to make amends for a wrongdoing
A punishment imposed by an authority for an offense, as the evidence showed
A promise made not to repeat the same mistake
An apology given in response to a complaint
quiz.answer: An action performed to make amends for a wrongdoing
quiz.explanation:
Penance is an act performed to show remorse and make amends for a wrong.
• penance = a self-imposed act of atonement; doing something to compensate
• From Latin: 'paenitentia' — repentance
• In religion: penance is assigned by a confessor; informally: any self-imposed reparation

quiz.question: The landlord was responsible for the maintenance of the building. What does 'maintenance' mean?
options: The insurance cover taken out to protect the building
The upkeep and regular repair needed to keep something in good condition
The legal ownership and liability attached to a property
The administrative duties associated with managing tenants, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The upkeep and regular repair needed to keep something in good condition
quiz.explanation:
Maintenance means the process of keeping something in good condition through regular care.
• maintenance = the work needed to keep something functioning properly
• From Latin: 'manutenere' — to hold in the hand; to keep
• 'Maintenance costs', 'road maintenance' — ongoing effort to preserve condition

quiz.question: The headteacher said she could not countenance cheating in any form. What does 'countenance' mean here?
options: To document or officially report the behavior
To permit or tolerate without objection
To discuss or address the problem openly
To punish or take action against the person responsible
quiz.answer: To permit or tolerate without objection
quiz.explanation:
To countenance means to accept or tolerate — to give approval or consent to.
• countenance = to approve of or tolerate; to give sanction to
• From Old French: 'contenance' — bearing, behavior
• As a noun: 'countenance' = a person's facial expression; 'lose countenance' = lose composure

quiz.question: The stranded hikers survived for three days on whatever sustenance they could find. What is sustenance?
options: Physical strength conserved through rest and shelter
Food and drink that sustains life
The mental resolve needed to survive a difficult situation
Water collected from rain and natural sources
quiz.answer: Food and drink that sustains life
quiz.explanation:
Sustenance is food and drink that keeps a person alive and functioning.
• sustenance = nourishment; the food and drink needed to maintain life
• From Latin: 'sustinere' — to hold up, sustain
• Also figurative: 'emotional sustenance' = what feeds the spirit

quiz.question: The auction house could not establish the provenance of the painting. What is provenance?
options: The estimated market value of a work at current prices
The history of a work's origin and ownership over time
The technical authentication of a work as genuine
The condition report for a work before sale
quiz.answer: The history of a work's origin and ownership over time
quiz.explanation:
Provenance is the record of origin and ownership history — where something came from.
• provenance = the origin and ownership history of an object
• From French: 'provenir' — to come from
• Crucial for art and antiques: unknown provenance raises questions about legality

quiz.question: She could not hide her repugnance when the topic was raised. What is repugnance?
options: A strong, instinctive physical or moral disgust
A reluctance to engage with a difficult subject
A considered ethical objection to a principle
An instinctive resistance to something new or unfamiliar
quiz.answer: A strong, instinctive physical or moral disgust
quiz.explanation:
Repugnance is intense disgust — a deep feeling of revulsion.
• repugnance = strong disgust; aversion
• From Latin: 'repugnare' — to fight against
• 'Moral repugnance' = finding something ethically intolerable; 'repugnant' = disgusting

quiz.question: The city passed an ordinance banning smoking in all public parks. What is an ordinance?
options: A national law passed by the central government
A local law or regulation enacted by a municipal authority
A bylaw agreed between neighboring municipalities, as the evidence showed
A code of conduct issued by an industry body
quiz.answer: A local law or regulation enacted by a municipal authority
quiz.explanation:
An ordinance is a local law or regulation — typically passed by a city or municipality.
• ordinance = a rule or law made by a local authority
• From Latin: 'ordinare' — to put in order
• Note: 'ordinance' (a law) vs. 'ordnance' (weapons) — easily confused

quiz.question: The critic praised the consonance between the film's visual style and its score. What is consonance?
options: A pleasing contrast between two elements of a work
A pleasing agreement or harmony between different elements
A recurring motif that unifies the work's different parts, as the evidence showed
A balance between emotional and intellectual content
quiz.answer: A pleasing agreement or harmony between different elements
quiz.explanation:
Consonance is a pleasing agreement between elements — harmony, not conflict.
• consonance = agreement; a harmonious combination
• From Latin: 'consonare' — to sound together
• Opposite: dissonance; in poetry: consonance = repeated consonant sounds

quiz.question: Poor governance at the charity led to the misuse of donated funds. What is governance?
options: The financial controls put in place to protect public funds
The system of rules and processes by which an organization is controlled
The leadership team responsible for running the organization, as the evidence showed
The legal framework within which an organization operates
quiz.answer: The system of rules and processes by which an organization is controlled
quiz.explanation:
Governance is the system of rules, practices, and processes by which an organization is controlled.
• governance = the act or manner of governing; the framework of oversight
• From Latin: 'gubernare' — to steer
• 'Corporate governance', 'good governance' — how power and accountability are structured

quiz.question: The creditor showed forbearance by not pursuing the debt immediately. What is forbearance?
options: A legal agreement to delay pursuing a debt
Patient restraint from pressing a claim or right
A financial arrangement to reduce payments temporarily
A formal waiver of a right to collect a debt
quiz.answer: Patient restraint from pressing a claim or right
quiz.explanation:
Forbearance is patient restraint — refraining from exercising a right one is entitled to.
• forbearance = the quality of being patient and restrained; holding back
• From Old English: 'forberan' — to endure
• 'Forbearance' is active patience — you could act but choose not to

quiz.question: The children's exuberance in the park was infectious. What is exuberance?
options: A boundless, enthusiastic energy and high spirits
The noise and activity typical of children at play
A happiness that comes from freedom and fresh air
An excitement caused by a specific game or activity
quiz.answer: A boundless, enthusiastic energy and high spirits
quiz.explanation:
Exuberance is overflowing energy and enthusiasm — lively, irrepressible high spirits.
• exuberance = enthusiastic energy; luxuriant abundance of spirit
• From Latin: 'exuberare' — to be fruitful, overflow
• 'Youthful exuberance' = the irrepressible energy and joy of youth

quiz.question: The engineer specified the tolerance for the component as plus or minus 0.1 millimeters. What does 'tolerance' mean here?
options: The maximum load the component can safely bear, as the evidence showed
The permissible range of variation from a standard
The minimum quality standard required for use
The margin of safety built into the design
quiz.answer: The permissible range of variation from a standard
quiz.explanation:
Tolerance (in engineering) is the allowable range of variation from a specified dimension.
• tolerance = the allowable deviation from a standard
• From Latin: 'tolerare' — to endure
• General sense: tolerance = willingness to accept difference; engineering: acceptable variation

quiz.question: The fragrance of the jasmine reached the guests before they entered the garden. What is fragrance?
options: A strong or distinctive smell of any kind
A pleasant, sweet smell
The scent produced by a flower in bloom
An aroma strong enough to be detected at a distance
quiz.answer: A pleasant, sweet smell
quiz.explanation:
Fragrance is a pleasant smell — a sweet or agreeable scent.
• fragrance = a sweet or pleasing smell
• From Latin: 'fragrare' — to smell sweet
• Distinct from 'odor' or 'stench' — fragrance is always pleasant

quiz.question: The hypnotist put his subject into a trance. What is a trance?
options: A state of deep sleep from which a person cannot be woken
A half-conscious state in which awareness of one's surroundings is reduced
A state of complete unconsciousness caused by suggestion
A heightened state of alertness produced by relaxation techniques, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A half-conscious state in which awareness of one's surroundings is reduced
quiz.explanation:
A trance is a semi-conscious state — between waking and sleeping, with reduced awareness.
• trance = a state of diminished consciousness; a daze
• From Old French: 'transir' — to pass over, to be seized with fear
• 'In a trance', 'trancelike state' — detached from normal awareness

quiz.question: Her entrance into the room silenced the entire party. What does 'entrance' mean here?
options: The doorway or opening through which she entered
The act and manner of arriving and coming in
The impact her appearance had on those present
The moment she was noticed by the other guests
quiz.answer: The act and manner of arriving and coming in
quiz.explanation:
An entrance is the act or manner of entering — with its full impact and effect.
• entrance = the act of coming in; a doorway; also to enchant (different stress: en-TRANCE)
• From Old French: 'entrer' — to enter
• 'Make an entrance' = arrive dramatically; 'the entrance' = the doorway

quiz.question: The official was removed for malfeasance in office. What is malfeasance?
options: Gross incompetence in carrying out official duties
Wrongdoing or misconduct by a public official
Corruption involving the misuse of public funds
Deception practiced on those in a position of trust
quiz.answer: Wrongdoing or misconduct by a public official
quiz.explanation:
Malfeasance is wrongdoing — especially misconduct by a person in an official position.
• malfeasance = illegal or dishonest behavior, especially by a public official
• From Old French: 'malfaisance' — wrongdoing
• Legal distinction: malfeasance (wrongful act) vs. misfeasance (lawful act done wrongly)

quiz.question: His complaisance made him easy to work with but difficult to respect. What is complaisance?
options: A willingness to please and comply, sometimes excessively so
A quiet, agreeable temperament suited to teamwork
A tendency to avoid conflict by finding compromises
A cooperative attitude that makes collaboration easy, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A willingness to please and comply, sometimes excessively so
quiz.explanation:
Complaisance is a willingness to please others — agreeable acquiescence, often to a fault.
• complaisance = obliging agreeableness; a desire to satisfy others
• From French: 'complaire' — to please
• Often slightly negative: too willing to agree; without sufficient backbone

quiz.question: The courtiers made obeisance before the queen entered the room. What did they do?
options: They bowed or made a gesture of respectful submission
They stood in a formal line to greet her arrival, as the evidence showed
They applauded as she entered the throne room
They moved aside to create a clear path for her
quiz.answer: They bowed or made a gesture of respectful submission
quiz.explanation:
Obeisance is a gesture of respect or submission — a bow or reverential act.
• obeisance = a bow or gesture of respectful deference
• From Old French: 'obeissance' — obedience
• 'Make obeisance', 'pay obeisance' — to show deference through a physical gesture

quiz.question: The soldiers carried out reconnaissance before the main force advanced. What is reconnaissance?
options: A preliminary survey of an area to gather information
A diversionary attack to draw the enemy's attention
A training exercise conducted before a real operation, as the evidence showed
A supply mission to prepare for a sustained advance
quiz.answer: A preliminary survey of an area to gather information
quiz.explanation:
Reconnaissance is a military survey of an area to gather information before an operation.
• reconnaissance = an exploratory survey to gather information
• From French: 'reconnaître' — to recognize, to survey
• 'Recon' = short form; now used in any context of preliminary investigation

quiz.question: The knight's puissance in battle was the stuff of legend. What is puissance?
options: The tactical intelligence he applied in difficult situations
Great power and might — especially in physical combat
The loyalty and devotion he inspired among his followers
The reputation he had acquired through years of service
quiz.answer: Great power and might — especially in physical combat
quiz.explanation:
Puissance means great power — strength and might, especially military or physical.
• puissance = great power; might
• From French: 'puissant' — powerful
• 'Puissant' = powerful (archaic adjective); in equestrian sport: a high-jumping competition

quiz.question: The committee's impuissance in the face of the crisis frustrated everyone. What does 'impuissance' mean?
options: Its slowness in recognizing the severity of the problem
Powerlessness — inability to act effectively
Its tendency to debate rather than decide
Its failure to communicate clearly with those affected
quiz.answer: Powerlessness — inability to act effectively
quiz.explanation:
Impuissance means powerlessness — the inability to exercise power or act effectively.
• impuissance = weakness; lack of power
• From French: 'im-' (not) + 'puissance' (power)
• Direct opposite of puissance

quiz.question: The government adopted a hardline stance on immigration. What is a stance?
options: A new policy introduced by the home secretary
A firmly held position or attitude on an issue
A set of proposals submitted to parliament
A position taken in response to public pressure
quiz.answer: A firmly held position or attitude on an issue
quiz.explanation:
A stance is a position or attitude adopted regarding an issue.
• stance = a way of standing; a firmly held viewpoint or attitude
• From Italian: 'stanza' — a stopping place; a standing
• 'Take a stance', 'change one's stance' — the position can be physical or metaphorical

quiz.question: The workers sent a monthly remittance home to their families. What is a remittance?
options: A letter written to report on their working conditions
A sum of money sent to someone in another place
A formal employment contract renewed annually
A portion of earnings set aside for future savings
quiz.answer: A sum of money sent to someone in another place
quiz.explanation:
A remittance is money sent to someone — especially from a worker abroad to family at home.
• remittance = the action of sending money; the money sent
• From Latin: 'remittere' — to send back
• 'Remittance economy' = an economy significantly supported by money sent from abroad

quiz.question: The volunteers worked long hours for a pittance. What is a pittance?
options: A token payment made as a gesture of appreciation
A very small sum of money — barely worth the effort
An irregular payment made when funds allowed
An amount sufficient for basic needs but nothing more
quiz.answer: A very small sum of money — barely worth the effort
quiz.explanation:
A pittance is a very small, inadequate amount of money.
• pittance = a very small or inadequate payment
• From Old French: 'pitance' — a monk's ration of food
• 'A mere pittance', 'paid a pittance' — the amount is contemptibly small

quiz.question: The translator struggled to capture the nuances of the original poem. What are nuances?
options: The cultural references that make a poem difficult to translate
Subtle distinctions of meaning or expression
The specific vocabulary choices that define an author's style
The emotional content that does not survive translation
quiz.answer: Subtle distinctions of meaning or expression
quiz.explanation:
Nuance refers to subtle shades of meaning — the fine distinctions that make expression precise.
• nuance = a subtle difference in meaning, tone, or expression
• From French: 'nuancer' — to shade
• 'A nuanced argument' = one that appreciates fine distinctions; opposite of blunt or simplistic

quiz.question: The union submitted a formal grievance on behalf of the dismissed workers. What is a grievance?
options: A complaint about unfair or unjust treatment
A legal claim for compensation after dismissal
A request for mediation between employer and employee
A petition signed by other workers in solidarity
quiz.answer: A complaint about unfair or unjust treatment
quiz.explanation:
A grievance is a formal complaint about unfair or unjust treatment.
• grievance = a real or perceived injustice causing resentment
• From Old French: 'grevance' — a burden, harm
• 'Air a grievance', 'grievance procedure' — a formal complaint demanding redress

quiz.question: The judge questioned the relevance of the new evidence to the case. What is relevance?
options: Whether the evidence had been properly authenticated
Whether the evidence had a bearing on the matter at hand
Whether the evidence could be admitted under the rules of court
Whether the evidence would be understood by the jury
quiz.answer: Whether the evidence had a bearing on the matter at hand
quiz.explanation:
Relevance is the quality of being directly related to and significant for the matter in question.
• relevance = the quality of being applicable and pertinent
• From Latin: 'relevare' — to raise, lift
• 'Relevant' = applicable to the matter; 'irrelevant' = having no bearing on it

quiz.question: The school required observance of the uniform code at all times. What is observance?
options: The monitoring of students' behavior by staff
The practice of adhering to a rule, custom, or ceremony
The formal acknowledgment of a school rule in writing, as the evidence showed
The system of reporting violations to the headteacher
quiz.answer: The practice of adhering to a rule, custom, or ceremony
quiz.explanation:
Observance means the practice of following a rule, custom, or ceremony.
• observance = the action of obeying or following a rule or custom
• From Latin: 'observare' — to watch over, comply with
• 'Religious observance' = practice of religious rituals; 'strict observance' = close adherence

quiz.question: She made allowances for his difficult childhood when judging his behavior. What does 'make allowances' mean?
options: To take mitigating circumstances into account when forming a judgment
To excuse behavior that would otherwise be unacceptable
To provide financial support for someone in a difficult situation, as the evidence showed
To consider a range of possible explanations before deciding
quiz.answer: To take mitigating circumstances into account when forming a judgment
quiz.explanation:
'Make allowances for' means to take mitigating factors into account — to judge less harshly given the circumstances.
• allowance = a sum given or a concession made; to make allowances = to consider context
• From Old French: 'allouer' — to assign, allot
• 'Allowance for error', 'make allowances' — adjusting one's expectations or judgment

quiz.question: The project was held in abeyance while funding was sought. What does 'in abeyance' mean?
options: The project was being reviewed and might be cancelled
The project was temporarily suspended but expected to resume
The project had been paused due to a legal challenge
The project was awaiting approval from a higher authority, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The project was temporarily suspended but expected to resume
quiz.explanation:
'In abeyance' means temporarily put aside — suspended, neither active nor abandoned.
• abeyance = temporary suspension; a state of not being in use or applied
• From Old French: 'abaier' — to gape at (yearning expectation)
• 'In abeyance' is not permanent abandonment — the matter remains open

quiz.question: She claimed clairvoyance and said she could see events happening in another country. What is clairvoyance?
options: A claimed ability to see things hidden or at a distance
An ability to predict future events with accuracy
A sensitivity to the emotional states of other people, as the evidence showed
A heightened perceptiveness gained through training
quiz.answer: A claimed ability to see things hidden or at a distance
quiz.explanation:
Clairvoyance is the supposed ability to see things beyond normal sensory capacity.
• clairvoyance = the purported ability to gain information through extrasensory perception
• From French: 'clair' (clear) + 'voir' (to see) — clear-seeing
• 'A clairvoyant' = a person who claims this ability

quiz.question: The judge said the court would take cognizance of the new evidence. What does 'take cognizance of' mean?
options: To formally admit new evidence at a late stage
To officially notice and take account of something
To request verification of evidence before accepting it
To instruct both sides to address the new information
quiz.answer: To officially notice and take account of something
quiz.explanation:
To take cognizance of something means to take official notice of it and consider it.
• cognizance = knowledge or awareness; judicial notice
• From Old French: 'conissance' — knowledge
• 'Take cognizance of' = formally acknowledge and give weight to

quiz.question: The museum's expansion was funded entirely by the munificence of a single donor. What is munificence?
options: Exceptional generosity — giving very large gifts
Wealth accumulated through a long career in finance
Funding provided on condition of naming rights
A bequest left in a donor's will after their death
quiz.answer: Exceptional generosity — giving very large gifts
quiz.explanation:
Munificence is exceptional generosity — giving on a grand and lavish scale.
• munificence = great generosity; lavish giving
• From Latin: 'munificus' — bountiful
• 'Munificent' = very generous; munificence = the quality of such generosity

quiz.question: The defendant maintained her innocence throughout the trial. What does 'innocence' mean here?
options: She claimed she had not intended to cause harm
She asserted that she had not committed the offense
She argued that she had acted under duress
She insisted the evidence against her was fabricated
quiz.answer: She asserted that she had not committed the offense
quiz.explanation:
Innocence means freedom from guilt — not having done the wrong act.
• innocence = the state of not being guilty; freedom from sin or wrongdoing
• From Latin: 'innocens' — not harming
• 'Presumption of innocence' = assumed not guilty until proven otherwise

quiz.question: The oil slick on the puddle displayed a beautiful iridescence. What is iridescence?
options: A mirror-like reflection of the surrounding sky
A lustrous, shifting play of rainbow-like colors
The sheen that forms on liquid surfaces in sunlight
A pattern of overlapping rings of color in a thin film
quiz.answer: A lustrous, shifting play of rainbow-like colors
quiz.explanation:
Iridescence is a lustrous, shifting display of rainbow colors.
• iridescence = a play of colors producing rainbow effects
• From Greek: 'iris' — rainbow
• Seen in oil films, peacock feathers, mother-of-pearl, soap bubbles

quiz.question: The incandescence of the hot filament produced the light. What is incandescence?
options: The emission of light as a result of being heated
The glow produced by a chemical reaction in darkness
The reflection of light from a highly polished surface
The electrical charge that builds up in certain materials
quiz.answer: The emission of light as a result of being heated
quiz.explanation:
Incandescence is the emission of light from a material heated to a very high temperature.
• incandescence = the glowing of a material caused by intense heat
• From Latin: 'incandescere' — to become hot
• An incandescent light bulb glows because its filament is heated by electricity

quiz.question: The morning sky had an opalescence that photographers came from miles to capture. What is opalescence?
options: A pale, milky appearance with shifting colors like an opal
A deep blue color that appears in the sky at dawn
The golden light produced at sunrise and sunset
A misty haziness that softens the colors of the landscape, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A pale, milky appearance with shifting colors like an opal
quiz.explanation:
Opalescence is a milky, iridescent appearance — shifting colors like an opal gemstone.
• opalescence = a milky iridescence showing shifting colors
• From 'opal' — the gemstone known for its shifting color play
• 'Opalescent' = showing milky iridescent colors; often describes sky, glass, or gemstones

quiz.question: The surgeon noted intumescence around the site of the infection. What is intumescence?
options: A swelling or expansion caused by internal pressure
A reddening of the skin indicating increased blood flow
A hardening of tissue at the site of an infection
A discharge of fluid at the surface of a wound
quiz.answer: A swelling or expansion caused by internal pressure
quiz.explanation:
Intumescence is a swelling or expansion — caused by internal pressure or heating.
• intumescence = the process of swelling up
• From Latin: 'intumescere' — to swell up
• Used medically (tissue swelling) and in fire safety (intumescent materials expand to block smoke)

quiz.question: The garden was at its florescence in late June. What is florescence?
options: The period of greatest flowering and blooming
The stage when flowers produce seeds and fruit
The process by which flower buds open in spring
The peak of growth before summer heat causes wilting
quiz.answer: The period of greatest flowering and blooming
quiz.explanation:
Florescence is the period of flowering — when a plant is in full bloom.
• florescence = the process or period of flowering
• From Latin: 'florescere' — to begin to flower
• Also figurative: 'the florescence of a culture' = its period of greatest vitality

quiz.question: The teacher asked the students to pay attention to the cadence of the poem when they read it aloud. What is cadence?
options: The rhyme scheme at the ends of the poem's lines
The rhythmic rise and fall of the voice in speech or verse
The pace at which the poem should be read to convey its meaning
The emotional tone the poem builds toward at its conclusion
quiz.answer: The rhythmic rise and fall of the voice in speech or verse
quiz.explanation:
Cadence is the rhythmic rise and fall of the voice — the musical quality of speech or verse.
• cadence = a sequence of notes or chords; the rhythm of speech
• From Italian: 'cadenza' — falling
• 'A closing cadence' in music = a chord sequence that creates a feeling of finality

quiz.question: The empire's decadence in its final years — luxury, corruption, and indifference — hastened its fall. What is decadence?
options: A period of excessive pleasure-seeking and moral decline
The military weakness that allowed enemies to advance
The economic mismanagement that drained the treasury
The political instability caused by frequent changes of leadership
quiz.answer: A period of excessive pleasure-seeking and moral decline
quiz.explanation:
Decadence is moral and cultural decline — excessive indulgence and falling standards.
• decadence = a state of decay; excessive self-indulgence and declining morals
• From Latin: 'decadere' — to fall away
• 'Decadent' = characterized by excessive luxury and declining morality

quiz.question: The scientist argued for the antecedence of the geological formation over the human artifacts found above it. What does 'antecedence' mean?
options: The greater importance of one thing compared to another
The priority of coming first in time
The certainty that one event caused another
The direct physical relationship between two layers
quiz.answer: The priority of coming first in time
quiz.explanation:
Antecedence means coming before — priority in time or order.
• antecedence = the state of preceding something else in time
• From Latin: 'ante-' (before) + 'cedere' (to go)
• 'Antecedent' = something that came before; antecedence = the fact of having come first

quiz.question: The discovery lent credence to the theory that the site was much older than believed. What does 'lend credence to' mean?
options: To create new doubts about a previously accepted theory
To make something more believable by providing support
To confirm something through a process of verification
To provide the final piece of evidence needed for certainty
quiz.answer: To make something more believable by providing support
quiz.explanation:
Credence is believability — the quality of being credible or worth believing.
• credence = belief in or acceptance of something as true
• From Latin: 'credere' — to believe
• 'Lend credence to' = make more plausible; 'give credence to' = believe or support

quiz.question: The incidence of diabetes has doubled in the past twenty years. What does 'incidence' mean here?
options: The severity of the disease among those who have it
The frequency of occurrence of a condition in a population
The speed at which the disease spreads between individuals
The proportion of the population currently living with the disease
quiz.answer: The frequency of occurrence of a condition in a population
quiz.explanation:
Incidence means the rate at which something occurs in a given population or period.
• incidence = the occurrence, rate, or frequency of a disease or event
• From Latin: 'incidere' — to fall upon
• 'High incidence of crime' = frequently occurring; in epidemiology: new cases per population

quiz.question: Financial prudence required setting aside reserves before spending on new projects. What is prudence?
options: Careful, forward-thinking judgment to avoid risk
A reluctance to invest even when conditions are favorable
A systematic approach to financial record-keeping
An awareness of one's legal obligations as a manager
quiz.answer: Careful, forward-thinking judgment to avoid risk
quiz.explanation:
Prudence is careful, sensible judgment — especially about practical matters and risk.
• prudence = wise caution; careful management of practical affairs
• From Latin: 'prudentia' — foresight
• 'Prudent' = showing good judgment; 'imprudent' = reckless and unwise

quiz.question: She specialized in environmental jurisprudence at law school. What is jurisprudence?
options: The practice of environmental law in the courts
The philosophy, theory, and science of law
The body of case law built up in a particular area
The study of international environmental agreements
quiz.answer: The philosophy, theory, and science of law
quiz.explanation:
Jurisprudence is the theory and philosophy of law — the study of law as a system.
• jurisprudence = the scientific study of law and legal principles
• From Latin: 'juris' (law) + 'prudentia' (knowledge, foresight)
• 'Medical jurisprudence' = law as applied to medicine; 'legal jurisprudence' = legal philosophy

quiz.question: The charity was founded to alleviate the indigence of families left homeless by the floods. What is indigence?
options: The distress and trauma caused by sudden displacement
A state of extreme poverty — having nothing
The hardship caused by lack of access to services
The long-term economic damage inflicted by a natural disaster
quiz.answer: A state of extreme poverty — having nothing
quiz.explanation:
Indigence is extreme poverty — a state of having almost nothing.
• indigence = a state of being very poor; destitution
• From Latin: 'indigere' — to lack
• 'Indigent' = very poor; 'indigent defendant' = a defendant who cannot afford a lawyer
